    Hamberger v. Eastman, 206 A.2d 239 (N.H.1964). This case is a chestnut in the American privacy
literature. See Turkington and Allen, Privacy Law, 539-540; Robert C. Post, Constitutional Domains:
Democracy, Community, Management (Cambridge: Harvard U. Press, 1995), 52.

    See above
227
    Samuel D. Warren & Louis D. Brandeis, The Right to Privacy, 4 Harv. L. Rev. 193 (1890); as
described in Harry Kalven, Jr., Privacy in Tort Law—Were Warren and Brandeis Wrong?, 31 Law &
Contemp. Problems 326, 327 (1966).

70
 a century of legal history, it amounts to little in American practice today. 228 The story of

the relative failure of Warren and Brandeis is precisely a study in how poorly continental

ideas do in the American climate.

In fact, it is best to think of the Warren and Brandeis tort, not as a great American

innovation, but as an unsuccessful continental transplant. For, though commentators

have failed to recognize it, what the two authors set out to do was precisely to introduce

the continental protection of privacy into America. It is hardly news that the Warren and

Brandeis was born in a world of Boston respectability closely akin to the high society of

late nineteenth-century Europe. Warren was a Boston Brahmin, a child of one of the

socially dominant families of the city. Brandeis was the son of Bohemian-Jewish

immigrants who had fled to America after 1848. 229 Their article was written in a fit of

outrage over newspaper reports of a party given by the Warrens, 230 and its main target

was the gossip pages of the “yellow press,” which Warren and Brandeis were convinced

represented a new phenomenon. 231 Like a number of authors of the period, 232 they were
